CURRENT EFFAIRS,

The “Irish’’ Times gloats over the proposal to rob
Ireland of twenty-two Parliamentary seats. It rubs its
hands with glee. We are not surprised; though in
the matter of the indecency of its unabashed anti-Irish
exhibition, it may live to regret that it was so candid.
There was joy in the Bigots’ Dust Bin on the 12th of
July. Ardilaun’s Orange Zzpress is likewise elated.
In the end, but we hope before it is too late, Irish
opinion generally will come round to our view that it
is practical humbug to keep up the pretence that the
classes that the Belfast News Letter, the Orange ZFz-
press, and to a great extent the Zrish Z'wmes stand for,
are Irish at all. The green Nationalists have been
crying “all creeds, all classes” until they are almost
black in the face from it, and under cover of this hum-
bug the anti-Irish Ascendancy has been attempting to
strengthen its hold on this country. Now we would
fight the Ascendancy all along the line. Instead of
attempting to appease the Orangemen we would regard
them as not being Irish at all and proceed accordingly.
The Redistribution proposals put all so-called Irish
Unionists to a searching test, and the test has shown
that they are enemies within the gates, that they are
anti-Irish, and that it is nonsense to continue to re-
gard them, as a body, as Irish at all. The “Irish’’
Times and the Ezpress gloat over this proposed further
robbery of Ireland. If the situation drives the Na-
‘tionalists back further on their own resources and opens
their eyes to the fact that it is the Ascendancy living
in, s0 many of them living on, the country who are the
chief enemy to be fought, the situation will not be
without its compensations.

The libeller of Canon MecInerncy says:— Apart,
however, from personal or party interests, the scheme
will appeal to the country generally as an honest and
impartial attempt to remedy some of the serious elec-
toral anomalies which hive grown up since the Re-
distribution Act of 1885.” It complacently remarks—
“The sweeping reduction of the Irish representation is
the most striking feature of the Government’s pro-
posals.’”

The Irish Times in the exuberance of its anti-Trish
gloating becomes quite facetious:—“Not unnaturally

they (the Irish Party) resent it decply, and they are
now presenting to the world the amusing spectacle ofra
Party whose raison d’étre is the destruction of the
Union, appealing with theatrical passion to the sanctity
of tho Act of Union.”” That is almost funny for Foz.
Further down the article the Zrish Z1'imes cannot re-
strain its nature, and the following, brimming with
good taste, is the result:—The effectiveness of the
Irish Party as a political engine will not suffer in any
way from the loss of twenty-two Nationalist seats.
Sixty members of Parliament can obstruct just as
vigorously as eighty, and can be supported for a good
deal less. We suspect that this latter consideration
will act as a potent private salve to the wounded feel-
ings of the Party managers.”

We think that the following fairly long quotation
from the Zrish Times is well worth the attention of our
readers : —“ Irish Unionists, or that large majority of
them who admit that Politics, like Theology, is a pro-
gressive science, and that to correct the working details
of the Act of Union is a very different thing from vio-
lating its vital principle, will find little fault with the
proposals of the Government. Many will regret, no
doubt, that the Resolution does not insist upon a
strictly proportionate reduction of thirty Irish seats,
and these can say with truth that the Nationalist poli-
ticians could not possibly have made any greater cla-
mour over thirty seats than they are now making over
twenty-two. To us the most serious flaw in the pro-
posals seems to be the neglect of the Government to take
advantage of an excellent opportunity of increasing the
Parliamentary representation of the Unionist miinority
in Ireland. The Ulster members represent not only
Ulster but the whole of Ireland. Their “ parish’’ is
the whole of Unionist Trealnd, as John Wesley’s parish
was the world. They have admittedly represented every
section of Irish Unionism on several recent occasions,
notably the Parliamentary agitation which led to the
exposure of the Devolution conspiracy. To have added
to the representation of the whole of Unionist Ireland
by giving a few new seats to Unionist Ulster would
have been a lawful concession to ‘considerations con-
nected with the character of the communities repre-
sented.” We might have expected also that a new
seat would have been added to Dublin City, and its
electoral boundaries so rearranged as to give the pros-
perous and intelligent loyalists of Dublin a voice in
the representation of the capital. As a whole, how-
ever, the scheme is practical, honest, and impartial.”

The Orange Fapress, from the offices of which Buff
emerges in the evening, opens its leading article on the
proposed robbery in this way:— The Redistribution
scheme which the Government supplied to the Press at
a late hour on Monday night is at least entitled to the
praise that in the principal point in which it differs
from all previous schemes of the kind it differs from
them for the better.’”” Further on we read:— There
will be disappointment in some quarters that the pro-
posals have not gone further and that the reforms ad-
vocated are not more drastic. Instead of a scheme for
tho removal we have one for the diminution of anoma-
lies. Ireland, should the Government carry its scheme as
outlined, will still be over-represented, though to a very
much smaller extent.’”” The following further quotation
from the Hzpress, a paper representing a section of our
« follow-countrymen,’” has an educational value:—
“Moro difficult to estimato is the criticism that must
fall upon the Ministerial scheme from the Unionist
standpoint, and particularly the disappointment that
many must feel at the representation of Ireland not
having been reduced to its proper proportionate limits.’

HE LEADER.

THE LEADER.

Now let us all shout “ tolerance ; let us send up the
catch cry that Ireland cannot afford to lose one of its
sons; let us denounce as narrow-minded bigotry the
‘nefarious LEADER that has been preaching that whilst
all in Treland are welcome as Irishmen that the plain
facts of the case are that, speaking generally and allow-
1ng for many exceptions both ways, the damned “Idola-
bors * represent Ireland, and the “Saved’” represent
anti-Ireland. Now that a proposal to rob Ireland of
twenty-two Parliamentary seats is hailed with jubila-
tion by the Belfast News Letter, the Irish T4mes, and
the Dublin Hapress, perliaps the substantial truth of
aur contention may dawn on the mind of the country.
We must and will have a nation; a nation made up
of all the inhabitants would be best; if certain classes
do not want an Irish nation, if they prefer to belong to
England, then in the name of common sense use our
eyes and recognise the fact; brush aside the English in
Ireland and go ahead making an Irish nation out of
those who desire an Irish nation. The sooner the
country generally comes over to our view the better for
the country. If those who want an Irish nation—and
generally they are the Irish “Idolators ”’—went about
making it, many of those who now do not want it, but
who have a constitutional aversion to being left out in
the cold, would by degrees find plenty of weighty
reasons why an Irish nation was desirable, and why they
should be Irish Nationalists.

The Belfast News Letter says :—Certainly Orangeism
has nothing to lose by looking back, for it is stronger
to-day than ever it was.”” We say that that is all the
more reason why the “Idolators’’ should at once orga-
nise to defend themselves against the machinations of
that secret society of which the man Coote, Justice of
the Peace of Tyrone, is a shining light.

In the excitement of the moment the Freeman seces
England’s Faithful Garrison in its true light as “ Trai-
tors within the Gate ’’—with which words it headed its
leading article on Thursday last. But these bursts on
the part of the Freeman are, like kopje-climbing, mere
spurts meaning about as much as a ““sthrong’’ resolu-
tion. The Freeman says:— The Irish nation is going
to have a new demonstration of the most malignant of
the evils inflicted upon it by the alien—the plantation
in Ireland itself of a faction that acknowledge no loyalty
to the country in which they have been born and by
which they live, and that are prepared on every occa-
sion which is offered to support the interest of the
foreigner against the interests of Ireland.” That is all
right as a “say,” but why does not the Zreeman join
with us in advocating a policy of “do”’ on these lines?
If England’s Faithful Garrison are traitors within the
gates, why not fight them? But we are too *broad-
minded.””” Ireland cannot afford to lose a single man,
and so forth is trotted out on every available occasion
by Nationalists who have not the hearts to be men.
What would the Freeman say to this proposition—*Ire-
land can afford to lose every one of its traitors within
the gates unless they change and throw in their lot with
the common fatherland?'”

The Freeman remarks : — There is no country in the
world that in such a crisis would have produced a simi-
lar breed of traitors; and the goneration and nurture
of the breed are the special curse of Castle corruption
and British rule in this country.”’ The I‘T’r.cemaﬂn is
somewhat unjust to Castle corruption and British xule;
we humbly suggest that in the breeding of these trai-
tors the *tolerance-proving’’ Nationalists, the tame
“Tdolators,”” the *timorous rodents ’’ whose 11!761"5
turned whito when the bigots barked at the Catholic
Association a year and a half ago—we humbly suggest
that all these must take part of the responsibility for
the breeding of the « Traitors within the Gates.”” Why

. ghould the traitors consider us when we evidently aro:
afraid to say “boo’’ to a bigot ? ;

, winds up' what we fear is oniy"va_ swag-
this way:— Mean fraud is as patent
cheme as it was upon the face of the

their countrymen will have no more' tl:uck with the sup:
porters of so base a fraud a.gai‘nsb, their country. They.
are standing in with the alien. against Irelu,pdl Be

so. Let Ircland sce that it stands true to itself, true
to its own interests, and apart and away from traitors

Now we say that the answer to the “ Traitors within g;he},

organisation for the defence of the civil interests of the

“ Tdolators >’ of this country. That is the way to’
answer the Irish Zimes, the Orange Lzpress, and othe:
who gloat over this new purposed robbery of Ireland. |

There wore no less than thirty-eight (38) columns of °
the Belfast News Letter of July 13th given over to the
Orangemen’s demonstrations and rantings and speeches
of the previous day!

In the course of a “ twelfth * oration the Rev. J. E.
Moffat, Grand Master of Dublin Orangemen, said :—
“Tt was good for those who were dwellers in the South
and West of Ireland to come to the North occasionally,
and witness the numbers, the inﬁuenoe,'a.nd'the euth:_x-
siasm of the Orangemen, and to bear in mind that in
their resistance to their treacherous enemies they had
the grand body of free and enlightened Orangemen be-
hind them (hear, hear). He could assure them that:

they who lived South of the Boyne needed all the sup- =

port they could get, for the Roman Catholics, under the -
malign and unchristian influence of the priests and the
Roman Catholic Association, were getting worked up to
greater intolerance—nay, hatred of Protestants—than
ever, and he did not hesitate to declare his firm belief:
that that hatred and contempt of protestants did not
always exist, and was the product of jealousy of the in-
dependence, prosperity, and general superiority of Pro-
testants. This state of feeling might not be manifest
upon the surface, but that it was working in all direc-
tions he had no doubt.”” This is the sort of stuff that
is fired like a torch-light procession down the throats of
the ignorant Orange rustics and factory hands by a ¢
“ Christian ’’ minister like Mr. Moffatt. Iow can one
wonder at the doings of the man Coote, of Tyrone,
when this incumbent of a Protestant chapel in Mil-
town, Dublin, talks like this to his ignorant dupes?
It is, no doubt, a fine holiday for this ranting, strife-
stirring Mr. Moffatt to leave his snug house in Rath-
mines—rated at £55—and go up to Belfast to slander
his neighbours in this = highly “ Christian’’ fashion
amidst the applause of the poor hinds of Orange Ulster.

The County Monaghan Orange Grand Master, Bro-
ther M. E. Knight, said :—“ There was one great thing *
about Orangeism, and that was that it afforded 2 com
mon platform on which Protestants of all denomina-
tions could come together for the promotion and ad- -
vancement of those common interests which were so
dear to them.”” Were it not about time that there was
some platform upon which “Idolators’’ could stand t
gether for the promotion and advancement of those
common interests which are so dear to them ? ;

In the course of a letter from the Bishop of Limerick
to a clerical school manager, Dr. O’Dwyer says:—“ T
have had already to complain publicly of the action of
the Catholic Commissioners in regard to these Rules;
but I must say that I did not think that outside of th
Trinity College Party on the Board the Commissiones
would exhibit so insolent a temper.  Whatever, ther
be the motive—deliberate and provocative high-hande:
ness, or the absence of any clear views in the minds of
the Commissioners—it is plain ‘that no information

At a meeting of the Munster Council of the Gaelic
League a letter was read from M. O’Higgins, of Dun-
garvan, in which he remarked:— There is no doubt
that the League would materially benefit by the estab-
lishment of Provincial Councils with certain powers,
such as (1) control of organisers; (2) general organisa-
tion; (3) spending of different allocated sums, cte.’’
And Mr. Goulding, in suggesting that the question of
establishment of Provincial Councils might profitably
be discussed at the Ard Feis, wrote:—The neced for
Combhairle na Mumhan is undoubted, and it would be
well if the other provinces were to follow the Munster
lead in forming such bodies.”” S

Industrially, the West cannot be said to be wide
awake. A Connacht correspondent connected with a
Connacht industry says in the course of a letter to us:—
““ Notwithstanding all that has been said and done, pre-
ference is still given to goods of English manufacture.
English goods displayed in shop windows and the sale
of them pushed hard from behind the counters of Irish
shops by Irish men and women! Some people imagine
that if Irish goods are displayed for one week out of
the whole year that-a great deal has been done to help
Irish manufacture.  Yes, they are sound Irish-Ire-
landers because they prefer English manufacture for
fifty-one weeks out of the fifty-two. Why do Irish-
born shop-assistants hide our Irish-made goods during
those 51 weeks? Why do they deny they have them
in stock until they find the customer is about walking
out? Why do they not even put them side by side with
English goods. All we want is fair play from our own,
for we are able to sell our goods on English and Scotch
ground, which seems our only hope of success.”” Con-
nacht badly wants some industrial organisation. In
view of the fact that Cork, Dungarvan, Kanturk,
Waterford and such places in the South have started
local industrial associations, we see no reason why simi-
lar bodies: should not be established in Sligo, Tuam,
Galway and other towns in the West.

Out of 413 Justices of the Peace in Ireland only 121
are Catholics. It is a pity these positions cannot be
thrown open to competitive examination, and the non-
Catholics might be able to secure about 121 of them.
As it is they hold 292. Those belonging to the Church
of England’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland hold 262 of
these honorary positions. If the position of Justice of
the Peace carried remuneration with it, the small num-
ber held by “Idolators’’ would no doubt be further
reduced by about seventy-five per cent.

The ratepayers of Dublin are triennially put to the
expense of electing one-fourth of their representatives

- to the Corporation and Poor Law Boards. The out-

going representatives may, or may not, be opposed ; bub
in the event of contests which must in many cases take
place, the machinery of the City Hall is at once put
in motion to secure that'the ratepayers shall have every
facility afforded them of recording their votes in the
most, expeditious manner. Houses (generally the public
schools) must be paid for, ballot boxes bought or reno-
vated, .polling booths erectéd, and even the sentiments
of Tsrael consulted by the purchase of the Old Testa-

“ment lest their prejudices might be offended by kissing

the New. Then a new staff of officials must be sought
for to record the voting, ranging from Returning Offi-

ccrs, Presiding Officers, Polling Clerks to messengers
and policemen. In whose hands lies the selection of
these: appointments, involving a large expenditure of
the ratepayers’ money, and to whom must application
be made to secure some of these coveted positions? If
rumour speaks truly, there is evidence that influence
(outside of the properly constituted authority) must be
sought to obtain any appointment, no matter how
humble. The favour of Alderman This or Councillor
That must be secured, and lucky is he who can command
such an influential body of opinion, particularly if the
former should condescend to act as sponsor. But what
do we find? At the recent election for Councillor for
the Mansion House Ward, two brothers—the sons of a
well-known cify Father and a large trader in the busi-
ness of Bung—were propelled into the positions of Pre-
siding Officer and Poll Clerk. One was barely out of
his teens—qualifying for a medical degree (hence we
suppose a three-guinea fee for him); the other, a mere
lad in knicker-bockers was polling-clerk to the aforesaid
medical at the reduced fee of one pound for the day.

Mr. Stanley Harrington of Cork, is soliciting votes
from the shareholders of the G. S. and W. Railway for
the post of one of the auditorships of that Company.
Mr. Harrington' is a Catholic, and whether he expects
any support on that head we do not know ; as he is one
of the Catholic Commissioners of National Education
concerning whom his Lordship the Bishop of Limerick
made such scathing references some time back, he would
have to show cause why he should not in equity be
classed as a “tame.”” In a circular which he has is-
sued, Mr. Stanley Harrington states that he has large
experience of Railway Accounts. As far as we know,
his experience in this dircction is drawn solely from a
six-mile ““railway ’’ called the Cork and Passage Rail-
way ; perhaps the mate of one of Guinness’s barges that
plies on the pellucid waters of the Liffey has a large ex-
perience of navigation! It will be interesting to note
if Brother Goulding will make a show of liberality in
backing up the candidature of this “Idolator *’ for the
post of audifor. If he does we would ask our readers to
calculate how much discount should be taken from the
“liberality ’* of Brother Goulding on the head of the
fact that Mr. Stanley Harrington, besides being a Com-
missioner of “ National ”’ Education, is likewise a direc-
tor of the Manure Company, W. and H. M. Goulding,
Ltd., of which the petulant bearer of the Inner Guard
Sword of the Free and Accepted Masons of Ireland is
chairman.

We are glad to note that Mr. M. Sullivan has raised
the question of the absorption of Irish railways by the
English companies. That, possible impending national
calamity has been allowed to drop out of public view
for some considerable time. Our readers will remember
that we dealt with it frequently some years ago. It
was difficult, however, to stimulate a keen public in-
terest in it, but now that a much greater watch is kept
on the affairs, and particularly on the directors of, at
least, some of our railways, the effort may be more suc-
cessful. Already the Midland of England has acquired
the Belfast and Northern counties. The Great Western
of England is putting more than one finger in the rail-
way pie associated with the name of Frederick C. Pil-
kington, of the National Assurance Company ; a director
of the London & North Western is on the Board of the
Dublin, Wicklow and Wexford. Mr. Sullivan comments :
‘ Nothing is more remarkable in these events than the
complete cffacement of our own Great Southern and
Western. A few years ago no railway in Ireland, and
few railways in England, stood in a higher position than
the Great Southern and Western ; now it cannot openly
say a word for or, against a proposed junction line from
its own Cork terminus—a line which, in the hands of
a great company that at some future period may be a
hostile company, must leave our Great Southern and
Western in a very weak position.”” We trust that at
the general meeting next month of the Great Southern
and Western, the Chairman will be questioned and
cross-questioned on this import_n.nt m_abter. Sy once the
English companics have the Irish railways safe in their "

claws, what cha.nf:e will the interests of Ireland an entity
have? Mr. Sullivan says:— The shareholders of the
Great S'ou.bhern and Western, before it is too late,
should insist on knowing how they stand. They should
know fully and precisely the ‘ engagemonts’ or™ under-
takings ’ with the Great Westorn of England and its off-
shoot, the ‘Rosslare and Fishguard;’ and thoy should
know exactly how their directors stand with regard to
the proposed ‘Loop Line’ at Cork. Theso things are
known to ¢ promoters ’ and speculators in London. Why
should they not be known to shareholders?’

The next general mecting of the Groat Southern and
Western Railway promisés to be a very interesting -one.
But the vigilance of the committee of shareholders
would be of little value without a considerablo amount
9f organisation by way of circularising. And circularis-
ing and issuing requests for proxies costs money. There
was not much change out of £90 when the sharcholders’
committee sent out requests for proxies, sent out a
reply to the petulant Chairman’s manifesto, and also
reports of the proceedings.  Men like Mr. Sullivan are
devoting a lot of valuable time and ability to the affairs
of this company in the interests of the shareholders, and
the shareholders who are not asked to go to any trouble
ought, at least, subscribe their share to the expenses.
If the widows, orphans, and others, who unhappily hold
shares in the National Assurance Company, subscribed
a few shilliiigs each a few years ago, and formed a share-
holders’ committee, there might not be so much cause
now for weeping and gnashing of teeth. This Great
Southern and Western Railway is a big concern that
wants watching. Shareholders who are in sympathy
with the work of the committee may send subscriptions
towards the incidental expense of carrying out the com-
mittee’s work to Mr. J. Donohoe, J.P., Abbey fouse,
Enniscorthy, or to Mr. M. Sullivan, LL.B., Belclare.
Temple Gardens, Dublin.

The Dairy Supply Co., Ltd., Belfast, have sent us

no credit on an Irish manufacturing firm with which
firm they wished to do business. The Belfast firm
wanted some stuff; the value of the particular order

order to an Irish firm—the only Irish firm we believe

pressing regret that the firm could not see their way

sult the order went to Manchester. We understand

too glad to quote for this class of goods, carriage paid.

THE SITGATION.

N four weeks more we will have entered upon our
I sixth year; but as this is our Summer Double:
Number, we avail of the special opportunity to do our
annual stock-taking in this issue.

During the first years we acted as artillery under cover
of which various units and bodies went to close quarters
with the many-sided enemy of the Irish nation. When
we started we had a clear, immediate purpose—our pur-
pose was to bring the most likely potential forces into
active work on Irish lines. We chose certain reliable,
trusty, and some heavy weapons, and we laid on with
vigour. We recked not whether what we did was
Art or Literature or “ class,”’ so long as it was honest,
and above all, effective. In the first place we had to
fight for the right of criticism, and that was no child’s
play when we started. We had to do that in order to
clear the way for a hearing for our views on the Indus-
trial Revival, the Language Revival, and the fight for
Tolerance, Justice, and Fair Play. We had to take the
false glamour off spurious respectability’’ and such
social artificial accretions that in the dark days of
Anglo-Trish decadence grew up in the social stagnant
pools. We attempted to raise up intelligence in what-
ever rank or walk of life it was to be found, and we
sought to place it in its proper high position in the na-
tional economy. Intelligence should be a great power in
all countries; the country in which anti-intelligence
crusts itself into various conventions is a country that
badly wants a few moral dynamite explosions.  Deeming
Ireland of the recent past to have been such a country,
we, after careful consideration and calculation, let off
a few moral infernal machines; and the pulse of the
country is now. much stronger, and the blush of health -
is coming back to its cheeks.

In a progressive country there is not much time for
looking back. We want to, get on with our national
efforts in the cause of Tolerance, Justice, and Fair
Play, and have now no space to waste weeping over
spilt milk. It is our conviction—a conviction for
which we will fight against all tomers as we have fought
—that an organisation for the defence of the temporal
interests of those who have the social, political, and
economic disadvantage of being Idolators’’ is an ab-
solute national necossity. We seek to invade no men’s
rights, but, as wo are men, we will guard and fight for
our own rights. It is bad for the State, as a whole,
that any part of it should be weak, and it is no good.

As one with a fairly extensive knowledge of the
retail tobacco trade, I was much interested in your
article of last week under the above heading, in which
58 you suggest various methods of fighting the British

combine in its efforts to capture the Irish industry.
d There is one aspect of the struggle to which I should

o your readers, and my remarks on the subject are based

v on personal investigation.

- In your article you state that “the Irish retailers

have backed the Irish manufacturers loyally. . . .

- In Dublin there are very few exceptions.”” I have no

doubt this is substantially true, if by retailers you mean

those commonly known as tobacconists, z.c., licensed

persons whose chief business consists in the sale of
smokers’ requisites, including, of course, tobacco in its

various forms. Now, for every tobacconist throughout

the country there are probably seven small gencral

55 < . dealers or hucksters, who are in many cases struggling

i women, and nearly always persons of meagre education.
I have visited the shops of hundreds of these in town
and country and never once have I found them stocking
Irish-made cigarettes, while in six cases out of every
seven I was astonished to see boxes of the British variety
on their shelves. No “shop ”’ seemed to be too small or
too remote to escape the attention of the Combine. In
some cases the shelves were packed with these boxes,
while the very meanest could gencrally produce one or
two even though they had little clse to offer. Millions
of these cigarettes are undoubtedly sold to Irish children
every year by hucksters whose ostensible business is the
‘vending of soap, paraffin, and candles. These people
- confine the application of the term “tobacco’ to roll
nd twist, which they rarely stock, so that when asked
if they sell tobacco,.they usually reply “No, but wo
sell cigarettes.”” 'The latter not being tobacco to their
, they not unnaturally conclude that no licence
 required to sell, them. In this reasoning they are,
find, ouraged by thé agents of the Combine, who
of course, mainly concerned in spreading the salo

I submit that this is a serious matter for everyone

ments is stunted by over-indulgence in these inferior

by this deception aided by the suggestive photographic
products of the “ British mind ** enclosed in the packets.
What is the remedy? I would suggest (1) that these.
people be compelled to drop the sale of cigarettes, or 4
take out licences, and thus ensure publicity ; (2) if they

to this official. The persons to apply the second remedy

vellers, who should leave no stone unturned to put a

Crom.—It would have been a crowning mercy had

Thousands of Idolators were delivered into my hands,
and I smote them with the edge of the sword even as
Joshua smote the Canaanites. ~ Those whom I drove
not to hell, I drove to Connacht. Yet I fear that I
have done the work of the Lord but negligently; His
abundant mercies I have not utilised to the best advan-

THE BALLYNOLAN ELECTION.

N the fulness of my conceit, and with a view to

rapidly ridding myself of all my spare funds, I
determined to “stand”’ for Empee, and for a rural
constituency—the Ballynolan division of the County
of Wicksford. I was an independent candidate, and I
stood upon lines so entirely my own that I now mostly
forget what they were, and it does not matter. Fow-
ever, I held a meeting in the largest available hall in
Ballynolan itself—a fine town, if it only had a few
manufactures, some factories, more people with “go”’
in them, and fewer people with pubs.  But ’tis an
imperfect world, and you can’t have everything. I
announced that after the meeting I would answer all
the questions that the electors and the non-electors
(who were present in great force) might wish to ask.
The object of this move was to enable me to get
through my speech without too many interruptions
from the free and independent non-electors, because,
how could they have the heart to interrupt, when they
might ask any questions they liked afterwards? It
was, I thought, a prudent move, independent candi-
dates not being, as a rule, persons whose candidature
is “ cordially endorsed ”’ by the local oracles of the
voice of the Irish people.”” Well, I got through my
big speech somehow—I don't quite #now how—and
wound up my peroration “in a lather of sweat’’ amid
a startling silence.  This did not promise too well.
My speech went down like a stone in a duck-pond, and
the weedy waters of inappreciative silence closed over
it at once. I resumed my seat, and mopped my noble
brow, leaving my chairman to make the announcement
that T would now answer any questions the audience
liked to ask. Silently taking stock of the audience
during this brief, welcome interval, I made up my
mind that I was not going to get in for the Ballynolan
division of Wicksford; they would be sure to elect the
Party’s official candidate, Dolan, a young lawyer of
whom nobody knew any harm, nor, for that matter,
anything at all. You see, it sometimes happens that
the best qualifications for winning the votes of the free
and independent electors are to be influentially recom-
mended, and personally unknown; Dolan was both,
and was sure to beat me by ten booths to two—or less,
even. No matter; I was “in for it’’ now, and as I
was not likely to win at the polls, there was nothing for
it but to take the worth of my money—for I was nomi-
nated already—out of the Ballynolanites in the form
of answers to their questions.

At first it looked as if nobody would avail himself of
the privilege of questioning me, which I had so gener-
ously thrown open to all, but at last one young fellow
rose, and nervously let off a question at me, stopping
in the middle of it to take off his cap. g

“ Are you in fayour (in his nervousness he said ¢in
faviour’) of the Hungarian policy?’

“No, young man,’”” said I, “I am not in faviour
of it.”

The schoolmaster grinned, for my questioner was a
pupil of his predecessor; I said young man all the more
severely because I saw at a glance that he was a non-
olector.

Ho reddened, and bridled up, and said:— What
have you to say against the Hungarian policy ¥’

Well, this book—a book written in English by Eng-
lishmen (I beg pardon, I mean Liftle Englanders) for
the British public—of which I want to say something,
purports to be the papers of the Patriots’ Club. That
especial club hangs out, I suppose, in London—that is,
if i6 ever corporealises itself—a thing I have doubts of—
and its province is the breeding of patriots, the teaching
of patriotism. This book is full of that word
““ patriotism.’”” In one essay it is written “in the old
days, when patriots still existed ’’—and in another we
find Patriotism defined as something more than Na-
tionalism (‘““which is Patriotism on the defensive, and
does not provide a constructive policy ’’) though still
one other essayist quotes Edmund Burke as saying, of
the spirit of Nationality, that it is “the impalpable
element, the inner life of States.”” Now, these two latter
definitions of Nationality can hardly be reconciled. An
element, to my mind, can never cease to be constructive
—every slab, every pile in the foundation of a house
never ceases to bear its load. It is inconsistencies like
these that lead me to believe that this club of patriots
has a literary rather than a corporeal existence.

The book consists of nine essays which deal with as
many aspects of the “ patriotic idea.” The first, which
deals with the “ idea ’’ in general is, to my mind, by far
the most interesting, and if I be tempted to quote some-
thing from it I do so under the impression that they
may help on the work that is speeding here at home.

general way the two “isms ’’ which are as nightmares in

IRISH IRELAND AND SCIENCE. -

DO not know if much attention is devoted by Irish
Irelanders to science in the larger sense. That is

to say, the science (or, strictly speaking, sciences) which
studios the laws of life, and the mysteries of the uni-
verse, aims to determine, or throw more light upon,
our essence and destiny, and incidentally considers the
part and meaning of national life in ~the universal
scheme, or movement, or whatever we elect to call it.
We have met worthy people who, if they were not
actually shocked at the thought of science, at any rate
fought shy of it. To certain of them it was a vague,
insidious something which would abolish both heaven
and fairyland;  to others it was the enemy of every
species of poetry; more had a dim idea that it treated
nationality as an old-fashioned dream. Obviously
those who think—if we may use so definite a word as
“think ”’—in any of these ways know very little about
real science. Doubtless in this, as in other regards,

we seem to be ablo to think for ourselves at last. ~ And

Lze.

we have suffered by our unfortunate contact with Eng-
land, which has set a host of superficial, quasi-scientific
notions in circulation.  Her unimaginative, imper-
fectly-informed, second-hand ‘ popularisers’’ of other
people’s science have done grievous harm to true science.
The theory of the Survival of the Fittest for a long
time provided fearful and wonderful material for many
of that tribe. They saw a blind, all-round war within
tho universe, and even poetry pictured “ Nature red in
tooth and claw.”” Some time ago I read a London
Professor’s study of “National Life from the Stand-
point of Science.”” He was troubled by this theory; he
kiiow enough to see that facts did not justify it; and
hesidos it played havoc with the ¢ Anglo-Saxon ** ideal ;

or if atom wars with atom, unit with unit, individual

* with individual, through all nature, the  Anglo-Saxon

Tace’’ as such cannot coalesce and survive. He shifted
the “struggle’ from the unit to the race or nation,
and his gloomy gospel was that nation must go on war-
Ting with nation in one arena or another, military or
commercial, and that a nation can only survive on the
wrecks of other and “inferior ’’ nations. He thus sub-
stituted one form of superficiality for another.

But science of the true kind, in England as elsewhere,
has latterly shown a saner and deeper temper, a finer
rgalisution of the character of her really wonderful pro-
vince.  This fact Has been illustrated plainly enough
in the most serious part of the discussion arising out of
some rather interesting experiments made by Mr. Butler
Burke, an Irish-born Professor of Cambridge. By the
application of radium to steriliséd bouillon Mr. Burke
produced what appeared to be living forms, though of a
primitive kind. Some scientists, of a kind, concluded that
here was spontaneous generation, that “dead matter
had produced living matter, and that we were within
measurable distance of discovering the origin of life.
Obviously, on the origin, mystery, and essence of life
such results, even if a thousand times more definite,

could throw no light whatever. The really interesting
point in the whole matter was to see how readily men
of any real scientific standing admitted this, and how
little store was set by the materialistic philosophy of
which we used to hear so much not very long ago. Mr.
Burke was told in some quarters that it is really quite
unscientific to speak of “dead matter’” at all, that
there is nothing of the kind in the universe, as shown
by the discovery of its amazing vibratory energy; that
the key-note of the whole universe is Life, that what
we call Death of any kind is simply a transmutation of
force. His sterilised bouillon was anything but “dead
matter.”” It seemed to be dead, but the radium was
able to stimulate info activity, into new form, the life
which is imprisoned therein as in all so-called dead mat-
ter. Yes, philosophic materialism seems to have passed
away—so far as really eminent scientists are concerned.

Indeed, in many ways the scientific outlook has been
revolutionised. The discovery, for one thing, of rva-
dium, with its astonishing and fascinating properties
and mysteries, has brought the scientist into a new
realm of wonder of which no one can see the end. Won-
der, indeed, has succeeded wonder in the scientific order
of late years. The ether discoveries, the Rontgen rays,
wireless telegraphy, and many more, might be instanced.
as illustrations of facts that are immensely en-
larging the whole conception of cosmic law. With
all, especially with the marvel of radium, even the
unimaginative scientist contemplating the universe has
been startled and fascinated. He has come to a border-
land whose facts transcend romance and magic.

The young Irish mind of to-day, I am much af):aid,

appreciate in any positive degree ‘the fascinating world

inches, even though his back was bent beneath its
humiliating burden. His eyes were resolutely fixed on
the gutter, but if they were lifted you would see that
they were blue and kind and gentle. Alas, they were
also wavering and watery, and the growing blotches on
the once delica*cly-cut nose beneath them confirmed
their confession of a fondness for the sup that had be-
come a passion.

Aloysius Gonzaga Mullally was the son of a respect-
able grocer in a small town in Leinster. I hesitate to
deseribe it more exactly. There are many Mullallys in
many towns in Leinster, and I shrink from indicating
precisely the branch of the house of Mullally of which
Aloysius Gonzaga was a scion. If asked of his parent-
age he would have spoken of his progenitor as a provi-
sion merchant—slipping hurriedly over “ provision ’—
and of “the mater "’ “as the daughter of a solicitor, the
sister of a barrister, and the niece of a dean. That is
the wdy of your Aloysiuses, a harmless way enough
within certain limits, and one that stimulates the quiet
humour of acquaintances. But sometimes it indicates
a fatal habit of mind; a fondness for titles as such;
confempt for hard and settled work in field or factory;
an undue pride in professions, with much else that goes
with this mental bent. And that is bad, both for your
Aloysiuses and the land that bears them. But to re-
turn to this one: he was, as I have said, the son of a
hard-working grocer, an honest, upright man, a little
aspiring and given to “ grandeur,” who was fated to
marry a lady with a contempt for trade. Mrs. Mul-
lally grew up in her professional atmosphere, and alas!
became ““stale’’ therein, for no doctor, barrister, or
attorney sought her hand or modest fortune. At thirty-
four, then, with ‘other matrimonial prospects growing
grey, she smiled upon Michael Joseph Mullally, and the
aspiring grocer became the proud husband of a lady
hitherto unsullied by contact with commerce.

Of this union Aloysius Gonzaga, and two girls who
were now nuns, nwsing evil-smelling half-breeds in a
torrid town in far-away Texas, were the offspring. Mrs.
Mullally h_a'd gonuine piety as well as pride, and her
groat ambition had been to see her son follow in the
foo(:_steps of her uncle, the dean. But Aloysius had no
not}'on that way, so his mother’s hopes and prayers re-
mained unfulfilled. Until the age of fourteen he was
a scholar at the Christian Brothers’ College in the town ;
then he was transferred to a very high-class College
near Dublin.  Stoneyhurst was talked of for awhile,
for Mrs. Mullally was equally anxious about his accent
and ‘the acquaintances he would make, but in the end
it was decided that a very superior Irish College would
do. And his career was not definitely decided upon.
Law, Medicine, or the Indian Civil Service would be
graced qnd honoured by Aloysius by-and-bye, when his
m%mahons were more settled.

saw in the coming time her handsome son, and he was
that, ruling millions of blacks in India, or adorning the
Bench at the Four Courts—or even beyond the sea.
‘Who knew? Such things had happened to other
talented Trish boys. And for ‘Anastatia and Madeline,
the girls, . there would be good matches with profes-
sional men, or, if God willed, convent life in some order
that only ladies entered. For her piety and her pride
were equal; a religious order wherein gentle women
did rough and menial work was never dreamt of by Mus.
Mullally for her daughters.

Alas for her might-have-beens! ’'Twas twenty yoars
and more ago since so she dreamed, and now she lies all
dreamless beside the green in Rathcolman Churchyard,
while her girls are ministering angels to Mexican half-
breeds in a Texan town, and Aloysius walks between
sandwich boards along a London gutfer.

The first shock came when Anastatia asked her
mother’s blessing on her resolve to go out to Texas.
There was always something good in the Mullallys;
even Aloysius, who had imbibed more of his mother’s
inflated pride than her daughters, was ever kind and
lovable beneath all his pretensions and affectations.
The girls had character and he had not, but all were
good at heart.  Mrs. Mullally dissented bitterly from
her daughter’s resolve, but to no purpose. Then within
a year Madeline determined to follow hey sister and her
Lord, and Mrs. Mullally began to feel as if the foun-
dations of her life were falling asunder. She would
not have begrudged her daughter to the cloister, but it
humbler her pride to think of them cleansing the sores
of semi-niggers in the hospital of an Order in which
many ‘“quite common persons’’ were enrolled.  But
Aloysius remained, and he, now grown a very shapely
lad of seventeen, came home on holidays with an' accent
and a taste in dress that soothed her pride and enlarged
her hopes. To be sure he had not done very well at his
exams., but he was always going to work hard and do
better, and a high-spirited, handsome young fellow
must not be driven over-much. So she told her own
heart, and so she insisted to Michael Joseph when he
began to express dissatisfaction. Once he half-ventured
to suggest taking his son from College, where the
youth seemed to be acquiring no more than an accent
and nice @sthetic tastes, but his spouse and more than
better half withered him with her glances and her scorn-
ful references To the trade by which both lived.

When Aloysius was eighteen, with his future all un-
decided, the worthy grocer took ill, and died within a
week. With his last breath, and with the clearness of
mind upon him which death somiétimes brings, he be-
sought his wife to keep their son at home, and make
him work at his father’s business. Humbled and awed

- by the shadow of eternity, and grieving that her hus-

band’s last houts should be troubled by not unnatural
fears, she made some sort of promise, but in her vory

while Aloysius Gonzaga, the child of so many hopes and

little from his Scottish acquaintance. But he got

steadily by his friends ab cards and by the bookmakers
to whom he began to resort. Mrs. Mullally sold the
Dbusiness in despair, feeling quite sure that even if }_aer
son deserted medicine for grocery it could only be with
disastrous results. She went to live in a little cottage
outside the town, and Aloysius, who always meant well,
resolved anew to work hard and trouble her less for
money.

He was twenty-one when his mother died, and
although he had occasionally got work at dispensing, or
as an unqualified assistant, he had taken no degree, and
knew in his heart of hearts that he never would. He
lost through drink the few precarious berths he got, and
each loss was followed by a month or so at home, during
which he would keep sober and make good resolutions.
To the last his mother believed in him, and inspired
him with fitful resolves. He was her darling boy, so
good to look at, so gracious in manner, so certain to be
successful in His profession, she was sure, when those
clusive degrees were won. I think that for her the sad-
‘mess of death fay only in the thought that when his
triumphant success came at last, as come it must, she
would not be with him to share it.

Thereafter came chaos and ruin irretrievable. With
the few hundred pounds he inHerited he returned to
Edinburgh, determined to justify that loved mother’s
hope and belief in him. But in a very little while work
began to pall, and whiskey to charm, and once more
Aloysius “trod the primrose path to the sound of
flutes.”” Tt was so easy and so agreeable while it lasted.
People liked him ; women were amiable and men genial.
It was nice to live pleasantly, and would have been nicer
still only for obtruding thoughts of the morrow. But
a little whiskey soon sent them packing, while Aloysius
idled and told himself that all was woll. Once or twice
before the money went he reflected that it would be a
sensible thing to invest it in a chemist’s business. He
could dispense, and there was, at least, a living to be
had that way. But the thought of being tied to a
shop, of serving pennyworths of this and ounces of that,
was hateful to his mother’s son, and the investment was
never made.
